channel.title: My New Hugo Site
channel.description: Recent content on My New Hugo Site

item.title: Bit-O-Asm-1/2/3/4
item.author: email@example.com (abund4nt)
item.description: Bit-O-Asm-1 We are provided with a disassembler-dump0_a.txt file. <+0>: endbr64 <+4>: push rbp <+5>: mov rbp,rsp <+8>: mov DWORD PTR [rbp-0x4],edi <+11>: mov QWORD PTR [rbp-0x10],rsi <+15>: mov eax,0x30 <+20>: pop rbp <+21>: ret Also the description helps us to better understand how to solve the challenge. (For all the following challenges it is the same, so I won’t put it again). Can you figure out what is in the eax register?

item.title: two-sum
item.author: email@example.com (abund4nt)
item.description: We are provided with a remote installation to connect to and the source code. When connecting we are asked for two numbers that satisfy the operation $n1 > n1 + n2 \text{ or } n2 > n1 + n2$. When entering random numbers it shows No overflow. $ nc saturn.picoctf.net 52830 n1 > n1 + n2 OR n2 > n1 + n2 What two positive numbers can make this possible: 1 1 You entered 1 and 1 No overflow Source code analysis #include <stdio.

item.title: You know 0xDiablos
item.author: email@example.com (abund4nt)
item.description: We are provided with a vuln binary and a remote instance to connect to, the binary has the following protections. Arch: i386-32-little RELRO: Partial RELRO Stack: No canary found NX: NX unknown - GNU_STACK missing PIE: No PIE (0x8048000) Stack: Executable RWX: Has RWX segments When it is executed it asks for an input and this is reflected when pressing enter. $ ./vuln You know who are 0xDiablos: test test Reverse engineering If we use cutter we will be able to obtain the code of the main function.

item.title: TryExecMe
item.author: email@example.com (abund4nt)

item.title: TryOverflowMe 1
item.author: email@example.com (abund4nt)
item.description: We are provided with a 64-bit binary, its source code and a remote instance to connect to. int main(){ setup(); banner(); int admin = 0; char buf[0x10]; puts("PLease go ahead and leave a comment :"); gets(buf); if (admin){ const char* filename = "flag.txt"; FILE* file = fopen(filename, "r"); char ch; while ((ch = fgetc(file)) != EOF) { putchar(ch); } fclose(file); } else{ puts("Bye bye\n"); exit(1); } } The binary has the following protections.

item.title: TryOverflowMe 2
item.author: email@example.com (abund4nt)
item.description: We are provided with a binary, the source code and a remote instance to connect to. int read_flag(){ const char* filename = "flag.txt"; FILE* file = fopen(filename, "r"); if(!file){ puts("the file flag.txt is not in the current directory, please contact support\n"); exit(1); } char ch; while ((ch = fgetc(file)) != EOF) { putchar(ch); } fclose(file); } int main(){ setup(); banner(); int admin = 0; int guess = 1; int check = 0; char buf[64]; puts("Please Go ahead and leave a comment :"); gets(buf); if (admin==0x59595959){ read_flag(); } else{ puts("Bye bye\n"); exit(1); } } The binary has the following protections.

item.title: Pentesting Mobile Setup
item.author: email@example.com (abund4nt)
item.description: Introduction The purpose of this post is to teach you how to set up a working environment from 0 to audit Android applications. We will start by installing Genymotion in our system, which will be our application emulator (it’s like having a phone inside your computer), we will configure the certificate with openssl and adb (to tunnel and intercept traffic with Burpsuite), then we will install Frida and we will bypass Instagram’s SSL Pinning using Frida.

item.title: Compress and Attack
item.author: email@example.com (abund4nt)
item.description: They provide us with a remote instance to connect to and the source code that encrypts the flag. To solve the challenge we must retrieve the value of the flag. #!/usr/bin/python3 -u import zlib from random import randint import os from Crypto.Cipher import Salsa20 flag = open("./flag").read() def compress(text): return zlib.compress(bytes(text.encode("utf-8"))) def encrypt(plaintext): secret = os.urandom(32) cipher = Salsa20.new(key=secret) return cipher.nonce + cipher.encrypt(plaintext) def main(): while True: usr_input = input("Enter your text to be encrypted: ") compressed_text = compress(flag + usr_input) encrypted = encrypt(compressed_text) nonce = encrypted[:8] encrypted_text = encrypted[8:] print(nonce) print(encrypted_text) print(len(encrypted_text)) if __name__ == '__main__': main() Source code analysis Create a compress() function that takes care of compressing text using zlib.

item.title: Hidden Message
item.author: email@example.com (abund4nt)
item.description: Hidden Message In this challenge we are provided with a remote instance to connect to, when we connect we get the encrypted flag $c$ plus its public key $n$ and public exponent $e$. $ nc 172.17.0.2 8888 Welcome to this challenge. Would you like to get the flag? (y/n) y {"message": "11E0809B25AE8FC1F1FF1459A10CA43FCA8E2375AA20DFCA356EA8829A208F14234DE2EE91376F248DA5581F899B44246F64E8CFB46462DF66D37FDE662C06889931B7B6100AD8E0435F37FFB0B150F448A4AABF7F33E967CADC6ABE8700DD60301EB84C3E4D68B908FED74BDBA1AEB925C1E952144AFD6D4E0BA420D6C8A967", "pubkey": ["6F48EF4905AD155E6659996AC53D910CCBA1D9BF52743EE022A62549051F497C074BD780CACB9E00D194C06D8EE0C1D4E037741EF38B9F788E06CDD2522075C942EFFEFAF58D4BA62DCA0E2C16ED25DD058E6C0D1B21EB98212154E636D4A75D452EDEE0417F32965E895948C24356F98F973FCB11EE4B7DF821E27BFF687957", "5"]} Once again we are dealing with an RSA cryptosystem. Vulnerability Since the public exponent $e$ is small and constant between messages we can use the Chinese remainder theorem to recover the flag in clear text $m$.

item.title: basic-mod1
item.author: email@example.com (abund4nt)
item.description: The challenge has the following description. We found this weird message being passed around on the servers, we think we have a working decryption scheme. Download the message here. Take each number mod 37 and map it to the following character set: 0-25 is the alphabet (uppercase), 26-35 are the decimal digits, and 36 is an underscore. Wrap your decrypted message in the picoCTF flag format (i.e. picoCTF{decrypted_message}) In summary we are told that each letter of the message is encrypted module 27, and the dictionary has the capital letters from A to B, has the numbers from 0 to 9 and an underscore (_).

item.title: basic-mod2
item.author: email@example.com (abund4nt)
item.description: The challenge has the following description. A new modular challenge! Download the message here. Take each number mod 41 and find the modular inverse for the result. Then map to the following character set: 1-26 are the alphabet, 27-36 are the decimal digits, and 37 is an underscore. Wrap your decrypted message in the picoCTF flag format (i.e. picoCTF{decrypted_message}) We are told that to solve the challenge we have to take each number modulo 41 and calculate the modular inverse to get the flag.

item.title: rsa-pop-quiz
item.author: email@example.com (abund4nt)
item.description: The following description is provided in the challenge. Description Class, take your seats! It's PRIME-time for a quiz... nc jupiter.challenges.picoctf.org 1981 A sentence inviting us to take a seat, supposedly they have a test prepared for us. We will use nc to connect to the remote server. Good morning class! It's me Ms. Adleman-Shamir-Rivest Today we will be taking a pop quiz, so I hope you studied. Cramming just will not do!

item.title: Math Typesetting
item.author: email@example.com (abund4nt)
item.description: Mathematical notation in a Hugo project can be enabled by using third party JavaScript libraries.
